# Dates {#sec-dates} ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: :::{.callout-important collapse = "true"} ## Penguin clean names dataset ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} penguins_clean_names <- readRDS(url("https://github.com/UEABIO/5023B/raw/refs/heads/2026/files/penguins.RDS")) ``` ::: ::: Working with dates can be tricky, treating date as strictly numeric is problematic, it won't account for number of days in months or number of months in a year. Additionally there's a lot of different ways to write the same date: * 13-10-2019 * 10-13-2019 * 13-10-19 * 13th Oct 2019 * 2019-10-13 This variability makes it difficult to tell our software how to read the information, luckily we can use the functions in the `lubridate` package. ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} library(lubridate) # library(tidyverse) ``` ::: :::{.callout-warning} If you get a warning that some dates could not be parsed, then you might find the date has been inconsistently entered into the dataset. Pay attention to warning and error messages ::: Depending on how we interpret the date ordering in a file, we can use `ymd()`, `ydm()`, `mdy()`, `dmy()` ## Reformat We can use functions in the `lubridate` package @R-lubridate to reformat dates written in different ways to *YYYY-MM-DD* format. ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} date("2017-10-11T14:02:00") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "2017-10-11" ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} dmy("11 October 2020") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "2020-10-11" ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} mdy("10/11/2020") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "2020-10-11" ``` ::: ::: Sometimes dates are presented in formats that do not translate cleanly into R. For example, the following date format isn’t converted correctly when we try to convert it to a date. ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} df <- tibble( date = c("X2020.01.22", "X2020.01.22", "X2020.01.22", "X2020.01.22") ) df |> mutate( date = as_date(date) ) ``` ::: ``` Warning: There was 1 warning in `mutate()`. ℹ In argument: `date = as_date(date)`. Caused by warning: ! All formats failed to parse. No formats found. # A tibble: 4 × 1 date 1 NA 2 NA 3 NA 4 NA ``` We can use `%` to be more explicit about what information is in each part of our date column, specifying where the 4-digit year (`%Y`), 2-digit month (`%m`) and 2 digit day (`%d`) are within each string. ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} df |> mutate( date = as_date(date, format = "X%Y.%m.%d") ) ``` ::: ## Extract Sometimes we might need to extract certain elements of a longer date-time value for summarising, filtering, or plotting data. ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} year("2017-11-28T14:02:00") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 2017 ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} month("2017-11-28T14:02:00") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 11 ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} week("2017-11-28T14:02:00") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 48 ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} day("2017-11-28T14:02:00") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 28 ``` ::: ::: ## Excel date formats Excel stores dates internally as serial numbers — counting the number of days since a specific “origin” date. - On Windows, the default origin is 1899-12-30 This means that sometimes the date is imported into R as the numeric (number of days since origin). The janitor package has a handy function `janitor::excel_numeric_to_date()` to deal with this. ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} library(janitor) excel_numeric_to_date(42370) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "2016-01-01" ``` ::: ::: :::{.task} ::::{.task-header} Your turn :::: ::::{.task-container} * [ ] **Question** What is the appropriate function from the above to use on the `date_egg` variable in the penguins dataset?
::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} penguins_clean_names <- penguins_clean_names |> mutate(date_egg = lubridate::dmy(date_egg)) ``` :::
Here we use the `mutate` function from `dplyr` to create a *new variable* called `date_egg_proper` based on the output of converting the characters in `date_egg` to date format. The original variable is left intact, if we had specified the "new" variable was also called `date_egg` then it would have overwritten the original variable. :::: ::: Once we have established our date data, we are able to perform calculations or extract information. Such as the date range across which our data was collected. ### Calculations with dates ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} penguins_clean_names |> summarise(min_date=min(date_egg), max_date=max(date_egg)) ``` ::: We can also extract and make new columns from our date column - such as a simple column of the year when each observation was made: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} penguins_clean_names <- penguins_clean_names |> mutate(year = lubridate::year(date_egg)) ``` ::: ### Filter dates We can filter datasets to include or exclude data from certain dates or date ranges. ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} # return records after 2008 plants |> filter(date_egg >= ymd("2008-01-01")) ``` :::
